questions.question: What is a secret trust, and why does it arise?

options.A: A trust that is created orally without any documentation, rendering it unenforceable at common law
options.B: A trust that exists outside the formal provisions of a will, where the testator secretly agrees with a named legatee that they will hold the bequeathed property on trust for a specified beneficiary
options.C: A trust created in a sealed envelope that the testator leaves with their solicitor to be opened only after death
options.D: A trust that is deliberately hidden from the Revenue Commissioners to avoid inheritance tax

questions.explanation: A secret trust arises where a testator wishes to benefit someone without publicly disclosing the arrangement. The testator leaves property to a named person in the will, having privately agreed with that person that they will hold it on trust for a specified beneficiary. Because wills are public documents, this mechanism allows a testator to provide for persons — such as an illegitimate child, a partner, or another person the testator does not wish to name publicly — without that arrangement appearing on the face of the will.

questions.question: What are the two types of secret trust and how do they differ?

options.A: Express secret trusts (where the testator tells no one) and implied secret trusts (where the beneficiary guesses the arrangement)
options.B: Fully secret trusts (where the will makes no mention of any trust) and half-secret trusts (where the will discloses the existence of a trust but not its terms or beneficiary)
options.C: Registered secret trusts (filed with the Probate Office) and unregistered secret trusts (kept entirely private)
options.D: Family secret trusts (for family members only) and commercial secret trusts (for business associates)

questions.explanation: There are two types of secret trust. A fully secret trust is one where the will makes no mention of any trust at all — it appears on its face to give property absolutely to the named person, with the trust obligation existing only in the private communication between the testator and the legatee. A half-secret trust is one where the will discloses that the named person holds the property on trust, but does not identify the beneficiary or the terms of the trust. In a half-secret trust, the existence of the trust is apparent from the will; its terms remain secret.

questions.question: Secret trusts appear to contradict the formal requirements for wills under Section 78 of the Succession Act 1965. What are the two theories that justify equity's enforcement of secret trusts?

options.A: The common law theory (that secret trusts are valid contracts) and the equity theory (that secret trusts are valid gifts)
options.B: The fraud theory (equity will not allow the Succession Act to be used as an instrument of fraud) and the dehors the will theory (secret trusts operate outside the will as independent inter vivos trusts)
options.C: The statutory theory (the Succession Act expressly recognises secret trusts) and the public policy theory (courts will always enforce private arrangements between consenting adults)
options.D: The estoppel theory (the legatee is estopped from denying the trust) and the unjust enrichment theory (the legatee would be unjustly enriched without the trust)

questions.explanation: Two theories justify enforcement of secret trusts despite the formal requirements of wills. The fraud theory, associated with McCormick v Grogan [1869] (Lord Westbury), provides that equity will not allow the Succession Act to be used as an instrument of fraud — if the legatee accepted the trust obligation, it would be fraudulent for them to take beneficially and deny the arrangement. The dehors the will theory, favoured in Re Young [1951] and Blackwell v Blackwell [1929], holds that secret trusts operate entirely outside the will as independent inter vivos trusts — the will is merely the mechanism by which property reaches the trustee.

questions.question: What are the three conditions required for a fully secret trust to be valid?

options.A: Writing, signature, and attestation by two witnesses
options.B: Intention, communication, and acceptance
options.C: Consent of the beneficiary, registration with the Land Registry, and acknowledgement in the will
options.D: Certainty of intention, certainty of subject matter, and certainty of objects

questions.explanation: For a fully secret trust to be valid, three conditions must be satisfied: (1) Intention — the testator must have intended to create a trust obligation on the legatee rather than make an outright gift; (2) Communication — the terms of the trust must be communicated to the legatee during the testator's lifetime; and (3) Acceptance — the legatee must have accepted the trust obligation, either expressly or by acquiescence. All three requirements must be satisfied for equity to enforce the secret trust.

questions.question: In Wallgrave v Tebbs [1855], a fully secret trust failed. Why?

options.A: The will did not comply with the formal requirements of the Wills Act
options.B: The testator never communicated any trust obligation to the legatees during his lifetime
options.C: The legatees refused to accept the trust after the testator's death
options.D: The beneficiary of the trust had predeceased the testator

questions.explanation: In Wallgrave v Tebbs [1855], the fully secret trust failed because the testator never communicated the trust obligation to the legatees during his lifetime. The requirement of communication is the critical condition for a fully secret trust — if the legatee is never told about the trust arrangement before the testator dies, there is no agreement and no fraud can be said to arise if the legatee takes the property absolutely. Without communication, the legatee is innocent and can take the property free of any trust obligation.

questions.question: In Moss v Cooper [1861], what did the court hold about silence in response to a communication of trust terms?

options.A: Silence is never sufficient to constitute acceptance — the legatee must expressly agree in writing
options.B: Silence after communication amounts to acceptance — the legatee who does not object after being told of the trust obligation is treated as having accepted it
options.C: Silence creates a presumption of rejection — the legatee is presumed to have declined unless they expressly accept
options.D: Silence is only treated as acceptance where the communication was made in the presence of a solicitor

questions.explanation: In Moss v Cooper [1861], the court held that silence after communication amounts to acceptance. Where the testator communicates the trust obligation to the legatee and the legatee does not object, equity treats the silence as acceptance. The legatee who hears of the trust arrangement and does nothing to reject it will be held to have accepted the obligation. This reflects the fraud theory basis of secret trusts — having accepted the obligation (even by silence), it would be fraudulent for the legatee to take free of it.

questions.question: In what crucial respect does the communication requirement for half-secret trusts DIFFER from the requirement for fully secret trusts?

options.A: Communication for half-secret trusts need not occur until after the testator's death
options.B: Communication for half-secret trusts must occur at or before the date of the will — not after it — whereas for fully secret trusts, communication can occur at any time during the testator's lifetime before death
options.C: Communication for half-secret trusts must be in writing, whereas for fully secret trusts it may be oral
options.D: For half-secret trusts, communication to any one of the trustees is sufficient; for fully secret trusts, all trustees must be informed

questions.explanation: This is the critical distinction between the two types of secret trust. For a fully secret trust, communication of the terms to the legatee can occur at any time during the testator's lifetime — before or after the will is made, as long as it is before the testator dies. For a half-secret trust, communication must occur at or before the date of the will. This requirement was established by the House of Lords in Blackwell v Blackwell [1929] and confirmed in Re Keen [1937]. Since the will itself shows the existence of a trust, later communication would be inconsistent with the will's own terms.

questions.question: Blackwell v Blackwell [1929] is the leading House of Lords authority on half-secret trusts. What did it establish?

options.A: That half-secret trusts are never enforceable in equity because they circumvent the formal requirements of wills legislation
options.B: That half-secret trusts are enforceable in equity, but communication of the terms must occur at or before the date of the will
options.C: That communication for a half-secret trust can be made at any time before the testator's death
options.D: That the dehors the will theory is the only valid justification for enforcement of secret trusts

questions.explanation: In Blackwell v Blackwell [1929], the House of Lords confirmed that half-secret trusts are enforceable in equity and upheld the fraud and dehors the will theories as justifications. Crucially, the House of Lords held that for a half-secret trust, communication of the terms to the trustee must occur at or before the date of the will. Communication after the will is made cannot satisfy the requirement because the will itself declares the property is held on trust — terms communicated after the will would be inconsistent with the document.

questions.question: Re Keen [1937] further developed the law on half-secret trusts by adding a condition about consistency of communication. What did the court hold?

options.A: That communication in a sealed letter given to the trustee before the will was made is equivalent to prior communication and is sufficient
options.B: That the communication of the trust terms must be consistent with the terms of the will — if the will refers to directions to be given in the future, prior communication cannot satisfy the requirement
options.C: That half-secret trusts are invalid unless the testator leaves a memorandum attached to the will
options.D: That communication by a solicitor on behalf of the testator is insufficient and must be personal

questions.explanation: In Re Keen [1937], the Court of Appeal held that the terms of the half-secret trust communicated to the trustee must be consistent with the terms of the will. In that case, the will referred to directions 'to be given' to the trustee, suggesting future communication — but the actual communication had been made prior to the will in a sealed envelope. The court held the trust failed because the prior communication was inconsistent with the will's own terms (which contemplated future instructions). This is sometimes summarised as the requirement of 'consistency between the will and the communication'.

questions.question: McCormick v Grogan [1869] is the leading authority on the fraud theory basis of secret trusts. What did Lord Westbury hold?

options.A: That secret trusts are void because they circumvent the Statute of Frauds
options.B: That equity enforces secret trusts to prevent the legatee from committing a fraud by using the statute as a shield to take property beneficially after having agreed to hold it on trust
options.C: That the fraud theory is the sole basis for enforcing secret trusts, and the dehors the will theory is incorrect
options.D: That secret trusts can only be enforced where the legatee committed an express fraud on the testator

questions.explanation: In McCormick v Grogan [1869], Lord Westbury held that equity enforces secret trusts on the basis of the fraud theory — that equity will not allow a statute (the Wills Act/Succession Act) to be used as an instrument of fraud. Where the testator communicated the trust to the legatee and the legatee agreed to hold on trust, allowing the legatee to take the property beneficially and deny the arrangement would amount to fraud. Equity therefore intervenes to enforce the secret trust and prevent the legatee from benefiting from their own unconscionable conduct.

questions.question: Re Young [1951] and Blackwell v Blackwell [1929] are associated with which theory of secret trusts?

options.A: The fraud theory
options.B: The dehors the will theory
options.C: The estoppel theory
options.D: The constructive trust theory

questions.explanation: Re Young [1951] and Blackwell v Blackwell [1929] are both associated with the dehors the will theory — the theory that secret trusts operate entirely outside the will as independent inter vivos trusts. Under this theory, the will is merely the mechanism by which the property is transferred to the trustee; the trust itself arises from the inter vivos agreement between the testator and the trustee. This explains why secret trusts are not subject to the formal requirements for wills — they are not testamentary dispositions but pre-existing inter vivos arrangements.

questions.question: Re Browne [1944] is an important Irish decision on secret trusts. What did the Irish High Court hold?

options.A: That secret trusts have no application in Irish law and must be disregarded
options.B: That the fraud theory applies in Ireland, and a fully secret trust where the legatee accepted the obligation during the testator's lifetime will be enforced by the Irish courts
options.C: That the dehors the will theory is the only basis for enforcement of secret trusts in Ireland
options.D: That secret trusts in Ireland require a formal deed to be effective

questions.explanation: Re Browne [1944] is the leading Irish decision confirming that secret trusts are enforceable in Ireland. The Irish High Court applied the fraud theory in enforcing a fully secret trust where the legatee had accepted the trust obligation during the testator's lifetime. The case confirms the availability of the remedy in Irish law and the application of the requirements of intention, communication, and acceptance — the same requirements as in English law.

questions.question: A testator leaves €50,000 to their friend Thomas in their will with no mention of any trust. Before making the will, the testator told Thomas: 'I am leaving you €50,000 but I want you to use it for the benefit of my partner, who I cannot name in my will.' Thomas said nothing in response. The testator has now died. What type of trust has arisen, if any?

options.A: No trust has arisen because the communication occurred before the will was made
options.B: A fully secret trust has arisen — the testator communicated the trust obligation to Thomas during their lifetime, and Thomas's silence constituted acceptance under Moss v Cooper [1861]
options.C: A half-secret trust has arisen because the beneficiary (the partner) is not named in the will
options.D: No trust has arisen because Thomas did not expressly agree in writing

questions.explanation: This is a fully secret trust scenario. The requirements are satisfied: (1) Intention — the testator intended Thomas to hold on trust for the partner; (2) Communication — the testator communicated the obligation to Thomas before death (it may be before or after the will in a fully secret trust); (3) Acceptance — Thomas's silence after communication constitutes acceptance under Moss v Cooper [1861]. The trust is a fully secret trust because the will makes no mention of any trust obligation — it appears to give the €50,000 to Thomas absolutely.

questions.question: A will states: 'I give €50,000 to my solicitor, to be held on trust for purposes known to her.' The solicitor was informed of the beneficiary's identity before the will was signed. Is this a valid half-secret trust?

options.A: No — the will acknowledges a trust and the terms were communicated after the date of the will
options.B: Yes — the will acknowledges the existence of a trust (half-secret), and communication occurred before the will was made, satisfying the requirement from Blackwell v Blackwell [1929]; the court must also verify consistency with Re Keen [1937]
options.C: No — the solicitor cannot serve as trustee because she drafted the will
options.D: Yes — there are no timing requirements for half-secret trusts

questions.explanation: This is a half-secret trust because the will discloses the existence of a trust but not its beneficiary or terms. The critical timing requirement from Blackwell v Blackwell [1929] — that communication must occur at or before the date of the will — is satisfied: the solicitor was informed before the will was signed. The court must also verify, per Re Keen [1937], that the communication is consistent with the will's terms. If the will refers to 'purposes known to her' (suggesting prior communication), this is consistent with the prior communication made. The trust is likely valid.

questions.question: What happens to a half-secret trust if communication of the terms occurs AFTER the date of the will?

options.A: The trust is still valid as long as communication occurs before the testator's death
options.B: The half-secret trust fails and the property is held on resulting trust for the testator's estate
options.C: The legatee may keep the property for themselves because the trust failed
options.D: The court will exercise its discretion to grant relief to the intended beneficiary on grounds of unconscionability

questions.explanation: For a half-secret trust, communication must occur at or before the date of the will (Blackwell v Blackwell [1929]). If communication occurs after the will is made, the half-secret trust fails — because the will already declares the property is held on trust, communication after the will is made is inconsistent with the will's own terms. When the half-secret trust fails, the property cannot be kept by the trustee-legatee beneficially (the will shows it was intended as a trust). Instead, it is held on resulting trust back for the testator's estate.

questions.question: Why is the timing rule different for communication in fully secret trusts compared to half-secret trusts?

options.A: Because fully secret trusts were invented later in legal history and the rule was never updated
options.B: Because in a fully secret trust, the will gives no indication of a trust, so communication at any time before death is compatible with the instrument; in a half-secret trust, the will already refers to a trust, meaning communication after the will would be inconsistent with the document
options.C: Because the Succession Act 1965 expressly provides different timing rules for fully and half-secret trusts
options.D: There is no difference in the timing rules — both types require communication before the date of the will

questions.explanation: The different timing rules reflect the internal logic of each type of trust. In a fully secret trust, because the will gives no indication of any trust, communication can occur at any point during the testator's lifetime — before or after the will is made — as long as it is before death. In a half-secret trust, the will itself already acknowledges a trust. Communication after the date of the will would be inconsistent with the instrument (as in Re Keen [1937]) because the will's own terms cannot be supplemented or amended by subsequent instructions that contradict or are inconsistent with the will.

questions.question: What is the 'dehors the will' theory and what practical consequence flows from it?

options.A: The theory that secret trusts are void because they operate outside the formalities required by wills legislation
options.B: The theory that secret trusts operate entirely outside the will as independent inter vivos trusts, meaning they are not subject to the formal requirements of the Succession Act 1965 for testamentary dispositions
options.C: The theory that all trusts created after a will is made are automatically invalid
options.D: The theory that the testator must use the words 'dehors the will' to create a valid secret trust

questions.explanation: The dehors the will theory (favoured in Re Young [1951] and Blackwell v Blackwell [1929]) holds that secret trusts operate outside the will entirely as inter vivos trusts. They are not testamentary dispositions — they arise from the agreement between the testator and the trustee during the testator's lifetime. The will is merely the mechanism of transfer; the trust itself predates and is independent of the will. The practical consequence is that secret trusts are not subject to the formal requirements of the Succession Act 1965 because they are not 'part of' the will — they operate as enforceable inter vivos trusts.

questions.question: A testator leaves property on a fully secret trust to two trustees, communicating the obligation to one of them but not the other. Can the trust be enforced against both?

options.A: Yes — once one trustee knows, the obligation binds both trustees automatically
options.B: The trust is enforceable against the trustee who was told but not, in some circumstances, the trustee who was not informed — the uninformed trustee may take their share free of the obligation if they were not told
options.C: No — the trust fails entirely because both trustees must be communicated with
options.D: Yes — a trustee who receives trust property is always bound regardless of their personal knowledge of the trust terms

questions.explanation: Where a fully secret trust has two trustees and only one is communicated with, the position depends on timing and the nature of the non-disclosure. The trustee who was communicated with is clearly bound. The trustee who was not informed did not accept any trust obligation. In some circumstances, the uninformed trustee may take their share free of the trust. However, if both trustees receive the property jointly and one was informed, equity may impose obligations depending on the circumstances. Candidates should note that the precise rules in this area are nuanced.

questions.question: For FE-1 exam purposes, which of the following is the most important distinction to draw when answering a secret trusts question?

options.A: Whether the secret trust involves land or personal property
options.B: Whether the trust is fully secret or half-secret, because the key requirements — especially the timing of communication — differ significantly between the two types
options.C: Whether the testator was domiciled in Ireland or another jurisdiction
options.D: Whether the trustee is a professional solicitor or a layperson

questions.explanation: The most important distinction for FE-1 purposes is whether the trust is fully secret or half-secret, because the requirements differ significantly. In particular, the timing rule for communication is the critical differentiator: communication can occur at any time during the testator's lifetime for a fully secret trust, but must occur at or before the date of the will for a half-secret trust (Blackwell v Blackwell [1929]; Re Keen [1937]). Candidates who fail to draw and apply this distinction will not earn full marks. They should also discuss both theories of enforcement (fraud theory and dehors the will theory).

questions.question: Where a fully secret trust fails (e.g., because communication never occurred), what happens to the property in the legatee's hands?

options.A: The legatee keeps the property for themselves absolutely, as the will gave it to them beneficially
options.B: The property is held on resulting trust for the testator's estate
options.C: The property passes to the intended beneficiary of the failed trust on the basis of unjust enrichment
options.D: The property is subject to a constructive trust in favour of the testator's next of kin

questions.explanation: Where a fully secret trust fails — for example, because the testator never communicated any trust obligation to the legatee — the legatee is innocent and takes the property absolutely. Because the will gives the property to the legatee with no indication of any trust, and no trust was ever communicated, there is nothing to prevent the legatee from taking beneficially. This is the basis of the fraud theory: without communication and acceptance, there is no fraud, and equity does not impose a trust on an innocent legatee who was never told of any obligation.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about secret trusts is INCORRECT?

options.A: A fully secret trust requires communication to occur during the testator's lifetime; communication after the testator's death is insufficient
options.B: Silence after communication of a trust obligation amounts to acceptance under Moss v Cooper [1861]
options.C: For a half-secret trust, communication can validly occur at any point during the testator's lifetime as long as it is before death
options.D: The dehors the will theory provides that secret trusts operate as independent inter vivos trusts outside the will

questions.explanation: Option C is incorrect. For a half-secret trust, communication CANNOT validly occur at any point during the testator's lifetime. The strict requirement is that communication must occur at or before the date of the will (Blackwell v Blackwell [1929]; Re Keen [1937]). Communication after the will is made will not satisfy the requirement for a half-secret trust and will cause the trust to fail. This contrasts with fully secret trusts where communication can occur at any time during the testator's lifetime (before or after the will), as long as it is before death. Options A, B, and D are all correct.
